revision.text:
'''宇治谷 孟'''（うじたに つとむ、[[1918年]] - [[1992年]][[11月5日]]）は、日本の[[歴史学者]]、[[国文学者]]。はじめて『[[日本書紀]]』の現代語訳を行った<ref>宇治谷（1988年、上）、カバー。</ref>ほか、『[[続日本紀]]』の現代語訳も担当した。
== 概要 == 1918年[[京都府]]生まれ<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">宇治谷（1995年）、奥付。</ref>。1941年に[[法政大学]]国漢科を、1943年に[[早稲田大学]]文学部を、[[1952年]]に[[立命館大学]]経済学部をそれぞれ卒業<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。[[清風高校]]等、幾つかの高校で教師を担任した後、最終的に[[滋賀文教短期大学]]教授および[[奈良芸術短期大学]]講師に就任<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。
早大在学中、歌人・国文学者の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、国史の現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。
1992年11月5日、交通事故死<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』の遺稿は、夫人の輝千代および兄の[[宇治谷義雄]]（[[愛知学泉大学]]教授）の整理・清書を経て発行された<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。
== 著書・訳書 == * 『日本書紀（上） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年。ISBN 4-06-158833-8。 * 『日本書紀（下） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年。ISBN 4-06-158834-6。 * 『続日本記（上） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1992年。ISBN 4-06-159030-8。 * 『続日本記（中） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1992年。ISBN 4-06-159031-6。 * 『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年。ISBN 4-06-159032-4。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[六国史]] * [[森田悌]] - [[歴史学者]]、[[群馬大学]]名誉教授。講談社学術文庫から、六国史の続きである『[[日本後紀]]』『[[続日本後紀]]』を現代語訳している。
{{DEFAULTSORT:うしたに つとむ}} [[Category:日本の歴史学者]] [[Category:日本文学研究者]] [[Category:日本の文学研究者]] [[Category:京都府出身の人物]] [[Category:1918年生]] [[Category:1992年没]]

'''宇治谷 孟'''（うじたに つとむ、[[1918年]] - [[1992年]][[11月5日]]）は、日本の[[歴史学者]]、[[国文学者]]。はじめて『[[日本書紀]]』の現代語訳を行った<ref>宇治谷（1988年、上）、カバー。</ref>ほか、『[[続日本紀]]』の現代語訳も担当した。
== 概要 == 1918年[[京都府]]生まれ<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">宇治谷（1995年）、奥付。</ref>。1941年に[[法政大学]]国漢科を、1943年に[[早稲田大学]][[文学部]]を、[[1952年]]に[[立命館大学]][[経済学部]]をそれぞれ卒業<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。[[清風高校]]等、幾つかの高校で教師を担任した後、最終的に[[滋賀文教短期大学]][[教授]]および[[奈良芸術短期大学]][[講師]]に就任<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。
早大在学中、[[歌人]]・国文学者の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、国史の現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。
1992年11月5日、[[交通事故]]死<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』の遺稿は、夫人の輝千代および兄の[[宇治谷義雄]]（[[愛知学泉大学]]教授）の整理・清書を経て発行された<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。
== 著書・訳書 == * 『日本書紀（上） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年。ISBN 4-06-158833-8。 * 『日本書紀（下） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年。ISBN 4-06-158834-6。 * 『続日本記（上） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1992年。ISBN 4-06-159030-8。 * 『続日本記（中） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1992年。ISBN 4-06-159031-6。 * 『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』 宇治谷孟訳、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年。ISBN 4-06-159032-4。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[六国史]] * [[森田悌]] - [[歴史学者]]、[[群馬大学]]名誉教授。講談社学術文庫から、六国史の続きである『[[日本後紀]]』『[[続日本後紀]]』を現代語訳している。

'''宇治谷 孟'''（うじたに つとむ、[[1918年]] - [[1992年]][[11月5日]]）は、日本の[[歴史学者]]、[[国文学者]]。はじめて『[[日本書紀]]』の現代語訳を行った<ref>宇治谷（1988年、上）、カバー。</ref>ほか、『[[続日本紀]]』の現代語訳も担当した。
== 概要 == 1918年[[京都府]]生まれ<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">宇治谷（1995年）、奥付。</ref>。1941年に[[法政大学]]国漢科を、1943年に[[早稲田大学]][[文学部]]を、[[1952年]]に[[立命館大学]][[経済学部]]をそれぞれ卒業<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。[[清風高校]]等、幾つかの高校で教師を担任した後、最終的に[[滋賀文教短期大学]][[教授]]および[[奈良芸術短期大学]][[講師 (教育)|講師]]に就任<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。
早大在学中、[[歌人]]・国文学者の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、国史の現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。
1992年11月5日、[[交通事故]]死<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』の遺稿は、夫人の輝千代および兄の[[宇治谷義雄]]（[[愛知学泉大学]]教授）の整理・清書を経て発行された<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。

'''宇治谷 孟'''（うじたに つとむ、[[1918年]][[8月5日]]<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> - [[1992年]][[11月5日]]）は、日本の[[歴史学者]]、[[国文学者]]。はじめて『[[日本書紀]]』の現代語訳を行った<ref>宇治谷（1988年、上）、カバー。</ref>ほか、『[[続日本紀]]』の現代語訳も担当した。
== 概要 == 1918年[[京都府]]生まれ<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">宇治谷（1995年）、奥付。</ref>。1941年に[[法政大学]]国漢科を、1943年に[[早稲田大学文学部]]を、[[1952年]]に[[立命館大学]][[経済学部]]をそれぞれ卒業<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。[[清風高校]]等、幾つかの高校で教師を担任した後、最終的に[[滋賀文教短期大学]][[教授]]および[[奈良芸術短期大学]][[講師 (教育)|講師]]に就任<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。
早稲田大学在学中、[[歌人]]・[[国文学者]]の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、自ら国史・現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。
1992年11月5日、[[交通事故]]により亡くなった<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』の遺稿は、夫人の輝千代および、兄の宇治谷義雄（[[愛知学泉大学]]教授）の整理・清書を経て発行された<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。
== 著作 == * 『日本書紀 全訳 現代文』 創芸出版（上・下）、1986年 * 『[[日本書紀]] 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]]（上・下）、1988年 *:ISBN 4-06-158833-8。ISBN 4-06-158834-6。 * 『[[続日本紀]] 全現代語訳』 [[講談社]]学術文庫（上・中・下）、1992年、1992年、1995年 *:ISBN 4-06-159030-8。ISBN 4-06-159031-6。ISBN 4-06-159032-4。 * 『晩香玉』 文芸社、2000年。ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 - 読みはわんしゃんゆい、恋愛小説
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[六国史]] * [[森田悌]] - [[歴史学者]]、[[群馬大学]]名誉教授。[[六国史]]の続きである『[[日本後紀]]』『[[続日本後紀]]』現代語訳を講談社学術文庫で刊行。
{{Normdaten}}

{{一次資料|date=2022-10}}'''宇治谷 孟'''（うじたに つとむ、[[1918年]][[8月5日]]<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> - [[1992年]][[11月5日]]）は、[[日本]]の[[歴史家|歴史学者]]・[[日本文学研究者|国文学者]]。はじめて『[[日本書紀]]』の現代語訳を行った<ref>宇治谷（1988年、上）、カバー。</ref>ほか、『[[続日本紀]]』の現代語訳も担当した。
== 概要 == {{一次資料|section=1|date=2022-10}}1918年[[京都府]]生まれ<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">宇治谷（1995年）、奥付。</ref>。1941年に[[法政大学]]国漢科を、1943年に[[早稲田大学文学部]]を、[[1952年]]に[[立命館大学大学院経済学研究科・経済学部|立命館大学経済学部]]をそれぞれ卒業<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。[[清風高校]]等、幾つかの高校で教師を務めた後、最終的に[[滋賀文教短期大学]][[教授]]および[[奈良芸術短期大学]][[講師 (教育)|講師]]に就任<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。
[[早稲田大学]]在学中、[[歌人]]・[[国文学者]]の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、自ら国史・現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。
1992年11月5日、[[交通事故]]により亡くなった<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』の遺稿は、夫人の輝千代および、兄の[[宇治谷義雄]]（[[愛知学泉大学]]教授）の整理・清書を経て発行された<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。
== 著作 == * 『日本書紀 全訳 現代文』 創芸出版（上・下）、1986年 * 『[[日本書紀]] 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]]（上・下）、1988年 *:ISBN 4-06-158833-8。ISBN 4-06-158834-6。 * 『[[続日本紀]] 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]]（上・中・下）、1992年、1992年、1995年 *:ISBN 4-06-159030-8。ISBN 4-06-159031-6。ISBN 4-06-159032-4。 * 『晩香玉』 [[文芸社]]、2000年。ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 - 読みはわんしゃんゆい、恋愛小説
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[六国史]] * [[森田悌]] - [[歴史学者]]、[[群馬大学]][[名誉教授]]。[[六国史]]の続きである『[[日本後紀]]』『[[続日本後紀]]』現代語訳を[[講談社学術文庫]]で刊行。
{{Normdaten}}

[[早稲田大学]]在学中、[[歌人]]・[[国文学者]]の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、自ら国史・現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。

He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese. == Overview..."
revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) * ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) * ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.
{{Normdaten}} [[Category:20th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:Japanese literature scholars]] [[Category:Professors at Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] [[Category:Lecturers at Nara College of Arts]] [[Category:Ritsumeikan University alumni]] [[Category:Waseda University alumni]] [[Category:Hosei University alumni]] [[Category:People who died in traffic accidents]] [[Category:People from Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1918 births]] [[Category:1992 deaths]] [[ja:宇治谷孟]]

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ** ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ** ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), pp. 480–483.</ref>.

The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), qq. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), qq. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), qq. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), qq. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), qq. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), qq. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.

He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
