revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}, into modern Japanese.
{{Normdaten}}

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学|12=draft}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|12=draft}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学|12=draft}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=draft}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学|12=draft}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学|12=draft}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂|12=draft}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学|12=draft}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|12=draft}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学|12=draft}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀|12=draft}}, into modern Japanese.
{{Normdaten}} <!--Ujitani Mitsuru--> <!--Ujitani Mitsuru--> <!--Ujitani Mitsuru--> <!--Ujitani Mitsuru-->

25 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宇治谷孟 up to rev 97759580
revision.text:
{{一次資料|date=2022-10}}'''宇治谷 孟'''（うじたに つとむ、[[1918年]][[8月5日]]<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994年）p.99</ref> - [[1992年]][[11月5日]]）は、[[日本]]の[[歴史家|歴史学者]]・[[日本文学研究者|国文学者]]。はじめて『[[日本書紀]]』の現代語訳を行った<ref>宇治谷（1988年、上）、カバー。</ref>ほか、『[[続日本紀]]』の現代語訳も担当した。
== 概要 == {{一次資料|section=1|date=2022-10}}1918年[[京都府]]生まれ<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">宇治谷（1995年）、奥付。</ref>。1941年に[[法政大学]]国漢科を、1943年に[[早稲田大学文学部]]を、[[1952年]]に[[立命館大学大学院経済学研究科・経済学部|立命館大学経済学部]]をそれぞれ卒業<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。[[清風高校]]等、幾つかの高校で教師を務めた後、最終的に[[滋賀文教短期大学]][[教授]]および[[奈良芸術短期大学]][[講師 (教育)#高等教育|講師]]に就任<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />。
[[早稲田大学]]在学中、[[歌人]]・[[国文学者]]の[[窪田空穂]]の授業を受けていた際、師の「日本の歴史を普通の人にも正しく理解してもらうために、『日本書紀』や『続日本記』を易しい現代語訳に訳したいが、自分にはその時間がない。自分のクラスから、意志を受け継ぐ人が出てくれれば嬉しい」という言葉が晩年まで心に残り、戦後何年経っても日本書紀の現代語訳が出ないため、自ら国史・現代語訳の決意を固めたという<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">宇治谷（1995年）、pp. 480-483。</ref>。
1992年11月5日、[[交通事故]]により亡くなった<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。『続日本記（下） 全現代語訳』の遺稿は、夫人の輝千代および、兄の[[宇治谷義雄]]（[[愛知学泉大学]]教授）の整理・清書を経て発行された<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />。
== 著作 == * 『日本書紀 全訳 現代文』 創芸出版（上・下）、1986年 * 『[[日本書紀]] 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]]（上・下）、1988年 *:ISBN 4-06-158833-8。ISBN 4-06-158834-6。 * 『[[続日本紀]] 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]]（上・中・下）、1992年、1992年、1995年 *:ISBN 4-06-159030-8。ISBN 4-06-159031-6。ISBN 4-06-159032-4。 * 『晩香玉』 [[文芸社]]、2000年。ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 - 読みはわんしゃんゆい、恋愛小説
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[六国史]] * [[森田悌]] - [[歴史学者]]、[[群馬大学]][[名誉教授]]。[[六国史]]の続きである『[[日本後紀]]』『[[続日本後紀]]』現代語訳を[[講談社学術文庫]]で刊行。
{{Normdaten}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:うしたに つとむ}} [[Category:20世紀日本の歴史家]] [[Category:日本文学研究者]] [[Category:日本の文学研究者]] [[Category:滋賀文教短期大学の教員]] [[Category:奈良芸術短期大学の教員]] [[Category:立命館大学出身の人物]] [[Category:早稲田大学出身の人物]] [[Category:法政大学出身の人物]] [[Category:交通事故死した人物]] [[Category:京都府出身の人物]] [[Category:1918年生]] [[Category:1992年没]]

== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[六国史]] * [[森田悌]] - [[歴史学者]]、[[群馬大学]][[名誉教授]]。[[六国史]]の続きである『[[日本後紀]]』『[[続日本後紀]]』現代語訳を[[講談社学術文庫]]で刊行。
{{Normdaten}}

During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, into modern Japanese.
{{Normdaten}}

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, into modern Japanese.
{{Normdaten}}

== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|ja|法政大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University|qq=}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waseda University|qq=}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|ja|立命館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsumeikan University|qq=}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|qq=}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|ja|滋賀文教短期大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|qq=}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|ja|奈良芸術短期大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara College of Arts|qq=}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Kuho Kubota|ja|窪田空穂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuho Kubota|qq=}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|ja|愛知学泉大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Gakusen University|qq=}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rikkokushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|qq=}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|ja|群馬大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunma University|qq=}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|ja|日本後紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kōki|qq=}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihon Kōki|qq=}}, into modern Japanese.
{{Normdaten}}

revision.text:
'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|qq=}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.

ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|qq=}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.

'''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|qq=}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|qq=}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.
{{translated page|ja|宇治谷孟|version=97759580|comment=Imported full ja history}}

He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. {{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|qid=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|qid=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|qq=}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.
{{translated page|ja|宇治谷孟|version=97759580|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:20th-century Japanese historians]]
[[Category:Hosei University alumni]]
[[Category:1918 births]] [[Category:1992 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]
}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|qid=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|qid=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|qq=|1=Tei Morita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540090}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|WD=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|WD=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|1=Tei Morita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540090}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|WD=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|WD=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|1=Tei Morita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540090}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.

He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|WD=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|WD=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Rikkokushi|Rikkokushi]] * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|1=Tei Morita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540090}} – Historian and professor emeritus at [[:en:Gunma University|Gunma University]], who translated later historical texts, such as [[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]] and [[:en:Shoku Nihon Kōki|Shoku Nihon Kōki]], into modern Japanese.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|WD=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|WD=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at [[:en:Hosei University|Hosei University]] in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at [[:en:Ritsumeikan University|Ritsumeikan University]] in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at [[:en:Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College]] and a lecturer at [[:en:Nara College of Arts|Nara College of Arts]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar [[:en:Utsubo Kubota|Kuho Kubota]]. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at [[:en:Aichi Gakusen University|Aichi Gakusen University]]<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇治谷孟]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1918|death_date={{death date|1992|11|5}}|birth_place={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|WD=Q201788}}|native_name=宇治谷孟|given_name={{ill|Tsutomu|WD=Q1439527}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449517]] Wikidata name: Tsutomu Ujitani [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsutomu Ujitani''' (宇治谷 孟, August 5, 1918<ref name="bukko">『現代物故者事典1991～1993』（日外アソシエーツ、1994th year ）p.99</ref> – November 5, 1992) was a Japanese historian and literary scholar. He is known for producing the first modern Japanese translation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}<ref>Ujitani (1988, Volume 1), cover.</ref> and also translated the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} into modern Japanese.
== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|en|Hosei University|WD=Q1323040|lt=Hosei University}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|en|Waseda University|WD=Q274486|lt=Waseda University}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|en|Ritsumeikan University|WD=Q1152190|lt=Ritsumeikan University}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|en|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|WD=Q7496290|lt=Shiga Bunkyo Junior College}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|en|Nara College of Arts|WD=Q6965235|lt=Nara College of Arts}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Utsubo Kubota|en|Utsubo Kubota|WD=Q1790933|lt=Kuho Kubota}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|en|Aichi Gakusen University|WD=Q4696569|lt=Aichi Gakusen University}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|WD=Q1151168|lt=Rikkokushi}} * {{ill|Tei Morita|ja|森田悌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tei Morita|1=Tei Morita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540090}} – Historian and professor emeritus at {{ill|Gunma University|en|Gunma University|WD=Q178017|lt=Gunma University}}, who translated later historical texts, such as {{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}} and {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|en|Shoku Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1041301|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}, into modern Japanese.
{{translated page|ja|宇治谷孟|version=97759580|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:宇治谷孟]]

He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|en|Hosei University|WD=Q1323040|lt=Hosei University}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|en|Waseda University|WD=Q274486|lt=Waseda University}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|en|Ritsumeikan University|WD=Q1152190|lt=Ritsumeikan University}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Seifu High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|en|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|WD=Q7496290|lt=Shiga Bunkyo Junior College}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|en|Nara College of Arts|WD=Q6965235|lt=Nara College of Arts}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Utsubo Kubota|en|Utsubo Kubota|WD=Q1790933|lt=Kuho Kubota}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|en|Aichi Gakusen University|WD=Q4696569|lt=Aichi Gakusen University}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.

== Overview == Tsutomu Ujitani was born in Kyoto Prefecture in 1918<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke">Ujitani (1995), colophon.</ref>. He graduated from the Department of Japanese Language and Literature at {{ill|Hosei University|en|Hosei University|WD=Q1323040|lt=Hosei University|1=Hosei University}} in 1941, the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Waseda University|en|Waseda University|WD=Q274486|lt=Waseda University|1=Waseda University}} in 1943, and the Faculty of Economics at {{ill|Ritsumeikan University|en|Ritsumeikan University|WD=Q1152190|lt=Ritsumeikan University|1=Ritsumeikan University}} in 1952<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />. After teaching at several high schools, including {{ill|Seifu High School|ja|清風高校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu High School|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清風|1=Q11561465|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561465}}, he became a professor at {{ill|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|en|Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|WD=Q7496290|lt=Shiga Bunkyo Junior College|1=Shiga Bunkyo Junior College}} and a lecturer at {{ill|Nara College of Arts|en|Nara College of Arts|WD=Q6965235|lt=Nara College of Arts|1=Nara College of Arts}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-okuzuke" />.
During his time at Waseda University, he attended lectures by poet and literary scholar {{ill|Utsubo Kubota|en|Utsubo Kubota|WD=Q1790933|lt=Kuho Kubota|1=Utsubo Kubota}}. Kubota expressed his hope for someone to create accessible modern Japanese translations of the *Nihon Shoki* and *Shoku Nihongi* to help the public better understand Japanese history. This inspired Ujitani to undertake the task himself years later, as such translations had not yet been produced<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki">Ujitani (1995), 11. 480–483.</ref>.
On November 5, 1992, Ujitani died in a traffic accident<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />. The manuscript for his *Shoku Nihongi (Volume 3): Complete Modern Japanese Translation* was completed posthumously with the help of his wife, Teruchiyo, and his brother, Yoshio Ujitani, a professor at {{ill|Aichi Gakusen University|en|Aichi Gakusen University|WD=Q4696569|lt=Aichi Gakusen University|1=Aichi Gakusen University}}<ref name="syoku-nihongi-atogaki" />.
== Works == * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全訳 現代文 日本書紀), Sōgei Publishing, 1986 (2 volumes) * *Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 日本書紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1988 (2 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-158833-8, ISBN 4-06-158834-6 * *Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Japanese Translation* (全現代語訳 続日本紀), Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992–1995 (3 volumes) ''' ISBN 4-06-159030-8, ISBN 4-06-159031-6, ISBN 4-06-159032-4 * *Wanshan Yue* (晩香玉), Bungeisha, 2000. ISBN 4-8355-0067-9 – A romance novel.
